project.name: wedding
project.description: Enterprise wedding industry management platform supporting CRM, order management, supplier management, event scheduling, and RBAC-based system administration.

nodes.name: __init__.py
nodes.summary: Empty package initializer for the API routing module.

nodes.name: auth.py
nodes.summary: Authentication API endpoints providing login, token refresh, logout, CSRF token generation, and current user retrieval with JWT-based session management and rate limiting.

nodes.name: login
nodes.summary: Authenticates a user by username and password, enforces rate limiting via Redis, and returns JWT access and refresh tokens on success.

nodes.name: refresh
nodes.summary: Refreshes JWT tokens by decoding the existing refresh token, validating it against Redis blacklist, and issuing a new token pair.

nodes.name: logout
nodes.summary: Invalidates the user's current session by blacklisting the JWT token in Redis.

nodes.name: event_handlers.py
nodes.summary: Event handlers for the finance module that react to order and approval events, managing receivable creation, status updates, refund approvals, and publishing finance domain events.

nodes.name: on_order_created
nodes.summary: Handles ORDER_CREATED events by creating a receivable record for the new order and publishing a RECEIVABLE_CREATED domain event.

nodes.name: on_order_status_changed
nodes.summary: Handles ORDER_STATUS_CHANGED events by setting the receivable due date when an order is confirmed.

nodes.name: on_order_cancelled
nodes.summary: Handles ORDER_CANCELLED events by updating receivable status and publishing a RECEIVABLE_STATUS_CHANGED event.

nodes.name: on_approval_approved
nodes.summary: Handles APPROVAL_APPROVED events for refund approvals, updating refund status, recalculating receivable amounts, and publishing refund and receivable domain events.

nodes.name: on_approval_rejected
nodes.summary: Handles APPROVAL_REJECTED events for refund requests, updating refund status to rejected and publishing a REFUND_REJECTED domain event.

nodes.name: register_finance_event_handlers
nodes.summary: Registers all finance event handlers (on_order_created, on_order_status_changed, on_order_cancelled, on_approval_approved, on_approval_rejected) with the event bus.

nodes.summary: PUT endpoint to update a user by ID. Delegates to UserService and logs changed fields. Requires 'system:write' permission.

nodes.summary: GET endpoint returning all roles. Requires 'system:read' permission.

nodes.name: handlers.py
nodes.summary: Domain event handlers that react to approval and schedule events, triggering order operations and user notifications via the event bus.

nodes.name: on_approval_approved
nodes.summary: Handles APPROVAL_APPROVED events by executing order cancellation or refund based on the approval type, using the publisher's DB session for transactional consistency.

nodes.name: on_approval_created
nodes.summary: Handles APPROVAL_CREATED events by querying active management users and sending them in-app notifications about the new approval request.

nodes.name: on_approval_resolved
nodes.summary: Handles APPROVAL_RESOLVED events by notifying the original applicant whether their request was approved or rejected.

nodes.name: on_event_created
nodes.summary: Handles EVENT_CREATED events by notifying the related salesperson that a planner has created a new schedule event for their order.

nodes.name: on_event_updated
nodes.summary: Handles EVENT_UPDATED events by notifying both the related salesperson and planner about the schedule change, avoiding duplicate notifications.

nodes.name: register_event_handlers
nodes.summary: Subscribes all domain event handlers (approval and event lifecycle) to the global event bus.

nodes.name: logging.py
nodes.summary: Provides structured JSON logging middleware and setup utilities. Includes StructuredLoggingMiddleware for request logging, a StructuredFormatter for consistent log output, a setup function for application-wide logger configuration, and the log_operation helper that persists user actions to the database.
nodes.languageNotes: Uses Starlette BaseHTTPMiddleware pattern with inline StructuredFormatter class for JSON log output. Sensitive query parameters are automatically redacted.

nodes.name: request_id.py
nodes.summary: Starlette middleware that generates a UUID-based request ID for every incoming request, stores it on request.state, and returns it in the X-Request-ID response header for distributed tracing.

nodes.name: order.py
nodes.summary: Core domain model definitions for the order module: Order, OrderItem, Payment, Contract, and Approval SQLAlchemy entities with their associated enums (OrderStatus, ItemType, PaymentMethod, PaymentStatus, ContractStatus, ApprovalType, ApprovalStatus). Defines table schemas with indexes and ORM relationships.
nodes.languageNotes: Uses SQLAlchemy 2.0 Mapped/mapped_column declarative style with TimestampMixin for audit fields. All enums inherit from str+enum.Enum for JSON serialization compatibility.

nodes.name: approval_service.py
nodes.summary: Business logic service for the approval workflow. ApprovalService handles listing approvals with user/order enrichment, creating approvals with APPROVAL_CREATED event publishing, and deciding approvals with APPROVAL_APPROVED/APPROVAL_RESOLVED event publishing. Includes the approval_to_dict serialization helper.

nodes.name: order_service.py
nodes.summary: Comprehensive order management service implementing the full order lifecycle: listing with scope-based filtering, detail retrieval with eager loading, creation with item validation and event publishing, updates with discount recalculation, status transitions with validation, payment recording with overpayment checks, contract upload with magic-byte validation, and PDF quote generation using ReportLab. Also provides serialization helpers.

nodes.name: apply.md

nodes.name: archive.md

nodes.name: bulk-archive.md

nodes.name: continue.md
nodes.summary: OpenSpec slash command that continues an existing change by creating the next artifact in the workflow sequence. Reads dependency artifacts and uses schema-driven instructions to generate proposal, specs, design, or tasks files.

nodes.name: explore.md
nodes.summary: OpenSpec slash command that enters explore mode for thinking through ideas, investigating problems, and clarifying requirements. Intentionally read-only -- never writes code, but may create OpenSpec artifacts to capture thinking.

nodes.name: ff.md
nodes.summary: OpenSpec slash command that fast-forwards through artifact creation, generating all artifacts needed for implementation in one continuous operation. Loops through the dependency graph until all apply-required artifacts are complete.

nodes.name: new.md
nodes.summary: OpenSpec slash command that starts a new change by creating a scaffolded directory and showing the first artifact template. Stops after setup, letting the user proceed with /opsx:continue to build artifacts incrementally.

nodes.name: onboard.md
nodes.summary: Guided tutorial slash command that walks a user through a complete OpenSpec workflow cycle on a real codebase task. Spans 11 phases from codebase analysis and task selection through implementation and archival, with pedagogical narration at each step.

nodes.name: propose.md
nodes.summary: OpenSpec slash command that creates a new change and generates all artifacts (proposal, specs, design, tasks) in one step. Similar to /opsx:ff but framed as a propose-and-build workflow entry point.

nodes.name: sync.md
nodes.summary: OpenSpec slash command that syncs delta specs from an active change into main specs. Performs intelligent agent-driven merging of ADDED, MODIFIED, REMOVED, and RENAMED requirement sections, preserving existing content not mentioned in the delta.

nodes.name: verify.md
nodes.summary: OpenSpec slash command that verifies implementation matches change artifacts. Checks three dimensions -- completeness (tasks and spec coverage), correctness (requirement mapping and scenario coverage), and coherence (design adherence and pattern consistency) -- producing a prioritized issue report.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: The README introduces the Wedding Management System -- an enterprise platform built with Vue 3 on the frontend and FastAPI on the backend, backed by MySQL and Redis. It covers the full wedding business lifecycle from customer acquisition through order management, event scheduling, and financial reconciliation. Start here to understand the system's purpose, architecture, and how to get the project running locally.

tour.title: Backend Entry Point
tour.description: The FastAPI application entry point assembles the entire backend: it registers all API routers (customers, orders, events, suppliers, finance, and more), wires up the middleware stack for CSRF protection, rate limiting, structured logging, Prometheus metrics, and JWT authentication. Reading this file gives you a complete map of every capability the backend exposes and the cross-cutting concerns that guard every request.
tour.languageLesson: FastAPI applications use a modular router pattern where each business domain gets its own APIRouter. Routers are mounted onto the main app with prefixes like /api/v1/orders, enabling clean separation of concerns and independent versioning per domain.

tour.title: Configuration and Database
tour.description: The configuration module uses pydantic-settings to manage environment variables for database URLs, Redis, JWT secrets, CORS, and file uploads. The database module creates an async SQLAlchemy engine with a FastAPI dependency for session injection. Together, these two files define the runtime environment that every other module depends on, making them the most-imported files in the codebase (database.py has the highest fan-in at 24 dependents).

tour.title: Core Domain Models
tour.description: The data layer defines SQLAlchemy ORM models for the primary business entities: Customer (CRM), Order (with items, payments, and contracts), Event (with venue resources and staff schedules), Supplier (with services and evaluations), and User (with roles and RBAC permissions). Each model includes enums for status workflows, relationships via foreign keys, and a shared TimestampMixin for audit timestamps. These models are the shared vocabulary that every service and API endpoint operates on.
tour.languageLesson: SQLAlchemy's declarative Base class is the registry for all ORM models. The TimestampMixin pattern uses mixin classes to inject created_at and updated_at columns into every model, avoiding repetitive column definitions and ensuring consistent audit trails across the domain.

tour.title: Order Lifecycle Service
tour.description: The order service is the most complex business module, implementing the full order lifecycle: creation with auto-generated order numbers, status state machines (draft, confirmed, completed, cancelled), payment recording, contract management, and scope-based access control (sales-only vs manager views). It demonstrates the layered pattern -- the API router handles HTTP concerns, Pydantic schemas validate input, and the service layer contains the business logic that coordinates models and events.
tour.languageLesson: FastAPI uses dependency injection (Depends) to provide database sessions, authentication context, and pagination parameters to route handlers. This pattern keeps route functions focused on HTTP concerns while delegating session management and authorization to reusable dependency functions.

tour.title: Event-Driven Architecture
tour.description: The event system provides the publish-subscribe backbone that decouples modules. The EventBus maintains a handler registry by event type, while event_types.py defines the centralized vocabulary of domain events (ORDER_CREATED, APPROVAL_STATUS_CHANGED, etc.). Event handlers react to these events -- for example, when an approval is approved, a handler automatically updates the related order's status. This pattern keeps modules loosely coupled while enabling complex cross-module workflows.
tour.languageLesson: Domain events decouple producers from consumers -- the order module does not need to know what happens when an order is created. It simply publishes an event, and any interested module (finance, notifications, etc.) can subscribe independently, enabling new features without modifying existing code.

tour.title: Finance Module (DDD)
tour.description: The finance module follows Domain-Driven Design with a strict layered architecture: domain entities (Invoice, Receivable, Refund, Transaction, Payment) in the domain layer, use-case orchestration services in the application layer, SQLAlchemy repositories in the infrastructure layer, and HTTP controllers plus event subscribers in the interfaces layer. This module is the most architecturally mature part of the codebase and serves as a reference pattern for how other modules should evolve.
tour.languageLesson: DDD layers enforce strict dependency direction: interfaces (controllers/subscribers) depend on application services, which depend on domain entities. Infrastructure repositories implement domain interfaces. This ensures business logic never depends on technical details like HTTP or database specifics.

tour.title: Authentication and RBAC
tour.description: Security is handled through a layered approach: the auth middleware extracts JWT tokens from requests and loads the user's RBAC permissions from Redis (with database fallback), the CSRF middleware validates tokens on state-changing requests, and the rate limiting middleware protects against abuse. The auth utility module manages token creation and refresh with configurable lifetimes. Together, these middlewares ensure every API request is authenticated, authorized, and protected against common web attacks.
tour.languageLesson: Starlette middleware in FastAPI processes requests in a pipeline. Each middleware can intercept requests before they reach route handlers and modify responses on the way out. The auth middleware uses the 'Depends' pattern within middleware to inject database sessions and validate JWT claims on every protected request.

tour.title: Frontend Application Shell
tour.description: The Vue 3 frontend uses Pinia for state management (auth store managing JWT tokens in sessionStorage), Naive UI as the component library, and a composable MainLayout with a collapsible sidebar, breadcrumb navigation, and user avatar dropdown. The frontend-architecture.md document provides a comprehensive guide to the route structure, state management patterns, and API integration layer that connects every Vue view to its corresponding backend endpoint.

tour.title: Key Frontend Views
tour.description: The frontend implements views for each business domain: the Dashboard displays KPI statistics, sales rankings, and conversion funnels; the Order views provide CRUD with dynamic item tables and contract uploads; the Customer views include a customer pool with recycling and transfer workflows; the Finance views cover receivables, payments, refunds, invoices, transactions, and reconciliation. Each view follows a consistent pattern of searchable data tables, modal forms, and status-driven UI elements.

tour.title: Infrastructure and Deployment
tour.description: Docker Compose orchestrates five services: the Nginx-served frontend, the FastAPI backend (with auto-migration on startup via docker-entrypoint.sh), MySQL 8.0, Redis 7, and an automated backup service with S3 upload support. The backend Dockerfile creates a secure non-root Python 3.13 image, while the frontend Dockerfile uses a multi-stage build to produce an optimized Nginx static image. Alembic manages database migrations, and nginx.conf handles SPA routing, gzip, and API proxying.
tour.languageLesson: The docker-entrypoint.sh pattern runs database migrations before starting the application server. This ensures the schema is always up-to-date when containers start, eliminating the need for separate migration deployment steps. The backup service demonstrates how to add operational utilities to the compose stack without modifying application code.

tour.title: Architecture Documentation
tour.description: The documentation layer provides a complete view of the system's design: the architecture document describes the layered Vue/FastAPI/MySQL stack and API conventions; the module-dependencies document maps cross-module data flows and the event-driven communication patterns; and the finance module's contracts and design-overview documents define the DDD boundaries, service interfaces, and the approval-integrated workflows. Together, these documents serve as the authoritative reference for understanding how the system is structured and why.
